atoms.content: An eigenvector is a special vector that only stretches or shrinks when a specific matrix is applied to it, without changing its overall direction.
An eigenvalue is the scalar number that tells you exactly how much the eigenvector was stretched, shrunk, or flipped.
Imagine stretching a rubber sheet. Most lines painted on it will curve and change direction. The few lines that stay perfectly straight but get longer are eigenvectors.
Diagonalization is rewriting a matrix strictly in terms of its eigenvectors and eigenvalues. This makes raising the matrix to a large power computationally trivial.

1. Eigenvectors don't change direction.
2. Eigenvalues measure the stretch factor.
3. Core math behind Principal Component Analysis (PCA).
If a transformation stretches a vector by a factor of 3 without rotating it, what is the eigenvalue? (Answer: 3)
